questions.questionText: You are building a canvas app that needs to display data from a SharePoint list containing 10,000 items. Users report that the app is slow. What is the most likely cause?
options: A. The SharePoint list has too many columns.
B. The app is not using delegation, causing Power Apps to retrieve all records locally.
C. The canvas app has too many screens.
D. The SharePoint list needs to be indexed.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. When a data source contains more records than the delegation limit (default 500, max 2,000), non-delegable functions cause Power Apps to only retrieve records up to the limit and process them locally. With 10,000 items, the app cannot efficiently retrieve all data without proper delegation.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. The number of columns affects performance but is rarely the primary cause of slowness with large record sets.
- C. The number of screens does not affect data retrieval performance.
- D. While SharePoint indexing helps, the primary issue is the delegation pattern in the Power Apps formula, not the SharePoint list itself.

questions.questionText: You need to create a custom table in Dataverse to store employee feedback data. Which type of table should you create if you want full customization capabilities including business rules, workflows, and security roles?
options: A. A virtual table.
B. A standard (custom) table.
C. An elastic table.
D. An activity table.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A standard custom table in Dataverse provides full customization capabilities including custom columns, business rules, cloud flows, security roles, and relationships. This is the appropriate choice for storing structured business data.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Virtual tables connect to external data sources and do not store data in Dataverse; they have limited customization.
- C. Elastic tables are designed for large-scale data with flexible schema but have limited support for business rules and security roles compared to standard tables.
- D. Activity tables are specifically for time-bound activities like tasks, emails, and phone calls, not general business data.

questions.questionText: You have a Projects table and a Tasks table in Dataverse. Each project can have many tasks, but each task belongs to only one project. What type of relationship should you create?
options: A. Many-to-many relationship between Projects and Tasks.
B. One-to-many relationship from Projects to Tasks.
C. One-to-one relationship between Projects and Tasks.
D. A lookup column in the Projects table pointing to Tasks.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A one-to-many relationship from Projects (one) to Tasks (many) correctly models the scenario where one project has many tasks, but each task belongs to only one project. This creates a lookup column in the Tasks table pointing to the parent project.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A many-to-many relationship would allow a task to belong to multiple projects, which contradicts the requirement.
- C. A one-to-one relationship would mean each project can only have one task, which is incorrect.
- D. A lookup in the Projects table pointing to Tasks would model the relationship backwards (project belonging to a task).

questions.questionText: You are building a canvas app that connects to a SQL Server database. Which type of license is required for users of this app?
options: A. A Microsoft 365 license is sufficient since SQL Server is a standard connector.
B. A Power Apps Premium license because SQL Server is a premium connector.
C. An Azure subscription is required instead of a Power Apps license.
D. No license is required for read-only access to SQL Server.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The SQL Server connector is classified as a premium connector in Power Apps. Any app that uses a premium connector requires users to have a Power Apps Premium (per-user or per-app) license.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Microsoft 365 licenses only cover standard connectors (like SharePoint, Office 365, etc.), not premium connectors like SQL Server.
- C. An Azure subscription may be needed for the SQL Server hosting but does not replace the Power Apps licensing requirement.
- D. All access to premium connectors requires appropriate licensing, regardless of whether it is read-only.

questions.questionText: You need a Power Automate flow to run every time a new item is added to a SharePoint list. Which trigger should you use?
options: A. Recurrence trigger set to check every minute.
B. 'When an item is created' trigger for the SharePoint list.
C. 'When an HTTP request is received' trigger.
D. 'Manually trigger a flow' trigger.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The 'When an item is created' trigger automatically fires whenever a new item is added to the specified SharePoint list. This is an event-driven trigger that is efficient and responsive.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A recurrence trigger polling every minute is inefficient, has delays, and wastes flow runs.
- C. An HTTP request trigger requires an external system to call the flow endpoint, not suitable for SharePoint list item creation.
- D. A manual trigger requires user interaction and does not automate the process.

questions.questionText: You want to display a list of products in a canvas app where users can scroll through items and tap on one to see details. Which control should you use?
options: A. Data table control.
B. Gallery control.
C. Form control.
D. Text label control.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Gallery control is the primary control for displaying a scrollable list of items in a canvas app. Each item in the gallery can have a custom layout with multiple controls, and you can handle the OnSelect event to navigate to a details screen.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. The Data table control shows data in a tabular format but has limited customization and interaction options.
- C. The Form control is for viewing and editing a single record, not for displaying a list.
- D. A text label displays static or dynamic text but cannot display a scrollable list of records.

questions.questionText: You need to create a view in a model-driven app that shows only active cases assigned to the current user. What should you configure?
options: A. A personal view with filters for status and assigned user.
B. A system view with filters for Status equals Active and Owner equals Current User.
C. A chart that filters by active status.
D. A dashboard with a filtered list.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A system view with appropriate filters (Status = Active and Owner = Current User) is the correct approach. System views are shared across all users and can use the 'Current User' dynamic filter for the Owner field, showing each user their own assigned cases.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Personal views are created by individual users and are not visible to other users or managed centrally by administrators.
- C. A chart provides visual representation but is not a primary view for displaying a filtered list of records.
- D. A dashboard can contain views and charts but the question asks specifically about creating the filtered view.

questions.questionText: You want to automatically set the Priority field to 'High' when the Category field is set to 'Critical' on a Dataverse form. What is the simplest approach?
options: A. Create a Power Automate flow triggered on record update.
B. Create a Dataverse business rule with the condition and set value action.
C. Write a custom JavaScript web resource.
D. Create a plug-in that runs on the pre-operation stage.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A Dataverse business rule is the simplest no-code approach to implement conditional logic on forms. You can create a rule that checks if Category equals 'Critical' and then sets the Priority field value to 'High'.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A Power Automate flow would work but is more complex and runs asynchronously, meaning the field would not update immediately on the form.
- C. Custom JavaScript requires coding and is more complex than a business rule for simple conditional field logic.
- D. A plug-in requires C# development and deployment, which is the most complex option for this simple requirement.

questions.questionText: In a canvas app, you want to navigate from Screen1 to Screen2 when a user clicks a button, and you want Screen2 to slide in from the right. Which formula should you use?
options: A. Navigate(Screen2, ScreenTransition.Cover)
B. Navigate(Screen2, ScreenTransition.None)
C. Back()
D. Navigate(Screen2, Fade)
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. The Navigate function with ScreenTransition.Cover makes the new screen slide in from the right, covering the current screen. This is the standard transition for forward navigation.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. ScreenTransition.None navigates without any visual transition effect.
- C. Back() navigates to the previous screen in the navigation stack, not to a specific named screen.
- D. While Navigate(Screen2, Fade) would work for navigation, the Fade transition does not create a slide-in effect from the right as requested.

questions.questionText: In a Power Automate flow, you need to send an approval email if the expense amount is over $1,000, otherwise auto-approve the expense. Which action should you use?
options: A. A Switch action based on the amount.
B. A Condition action that checks if the amount is greater than 1000.
C. A Filter Array action to separate high-value expenses.
D. A Do Until loop that waits for the amount to change.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A Condition action (If/Yes/No branching) checks whether the expense amount is greater than 1000. The 'Yes' branch sends an approval email, and the 'No' branch auto-approves. This is the standard approach for binary decision logic in flows.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A Switch action is for multiple possible values (like status codes), not for a simple greater-than comparison.
- C. Filter Array operates on arrays of data, not on a single expense record.
- D. A Do Until loop is for repetitive actions, not for conditional branching.

questions.questionText: Your organization has a REST API that is not available as a standard Power Platform connector. You need to use this API in your canvas app. What should you create?
options: A. A premium connector request to Microsoft.
B. A custom connector that defines the API endpoints, authentication, and operations.
C. A direct HTTP call from the canvas app using JavaScript.
D. A virtual table in Dataverse.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A custom connector allows you to define the API endpoints, authentication method, request/response schemas, and operations for any REST API. Once created, the custom connector can be used in canvas apps, model-driven apps, and Power Automate flows.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. You cannot request Microsoft to create connectors; custom connectors are the self-service solution.
- C. Canvas apps do not support direct JavaScript HTTP calls in the standard development model.
- D. Virtual tables connect to external data sources but require a Dataverse virtual table data provider, which is more complex than a custom connector for API access.

questions.questionText: You need to create a model-driven app form that shows different fields depending on whether the record type is 'Customer' or 'Vendor'. What is the best approach?
options: A. Create two separate tables for Customers and Vendors.
B. Use a business rule to show/hide fields based on the record type.
C. Create two separate forms and use form selector logic.
D. Put all fields on one form with no conditional logic.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A Dataverse business rule can show or hide form fields (and sections/tabs) based on the value of another field (like record type). This provides a dynamic form experience within a single form definition.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Separate tables would create data silos and complicate reporting across customers and vendors.
- C. While multiple forms with form selectors work, it requires users to manually switch forms; business rules provide automatic field visibility without user action.
- D. Showing all fields regardless of record type clutters the form and creates a poor user experience.

questions.questionText: You need to add a Status field to a table that has the values: New, In Progress, Completed, and Cancelled. The same set of values should be reusable across multiple tables. What should you create?
options: A. A local choice column on each table.
B. A global choice (option set) and use it as the column type on each table.
C. A text column with validation rules.
D. A lookup column to a Status reference table.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A global choice (option set) is defined once and can be reused across multiple tables. This ensures consistency of values and reduces maintenance when values need to be updated.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Local choices are defined per column and must be maintained separately on each table, leading to potential inconsistencies.
- C. A text column would not enforce the specific allowed values and would be prone to data entry errors.
- D. While a lookup to a reference table works, a choice column is simpler and more appropriate for a small, fixed set of values.

questions.questionText: You need to filter a gallery in a canvas app to show only items where the Title contains the text typed in a search box (TextInput1). Which formula should you use for the gallery's Items property?
options: A. Filter(Products, Title = TextInput1.Text)
B. Search(Products, TextInput1.Text, "Title")
C. LookUp(Products, Title = TextInput1.Text)
D. Sort(Products, Title, SortOrder.Ascending)
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Search function searches for records where any of the specified columns contain the search text as a substring. Search(Products, TextInput1.Text, "Title") returns all products where the Title contains the typed text.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Filter with = performs an exact match, not a substring search. Users would need to type the exact title.
- C. LookUp returns only the first matching record, not all matching records for a gallery.
- D. Sort reorders records but does not filter them based on search text.

questions.questionText: You need to create an approval flow where a purchase request must be approved by both the manager AND the finance director before proceeding. Which two configurations should you use? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
options: A. Use 'Approve/Reject - Everyone must approve' approval type with both approvers.
B. Use two sequential 'Approve/Reject - First to respond' approval actions.
C. Use a parallel branch with an approval action in each branch.
D. Set the approval to 'Custom Responses - Wait for all responses' with both approvers.
E. Use a single 'Approve/Reject - First to respond' with both approvers.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A and D. 'Approve/Reject - Everyone must approve' sends the approval to all listed approvers and requires all of them to approve before the flow continues. Alternatively, 'Custom Responses - Wait for all responses' achieves the same requirement with custom response options.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Two sequential approvals would work but are separate approval steps, not a single approval that requires both people. This is a valid alternative approach but not the most efficient.
- C. Parallel branches with separate approvals would send both simultaneously but each would complete independently without enforcing that both must approve.
- E. 'First to respond' only requires one person to respond, not everyone.

questions.questionText: You need to store a user's selected department across multiple screens in a canvas app. Which type of variable should you use?
options: A. A context variable using UpdateContext().
B. A global variable using Set().
C. A collection using Collect().
D. A local variable inside a formula.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A global variable created with Set() is accessible across all screens in a canvas app. This is appropriate for storing a single value (like a selected department) that needs to be referenced on multiple screens.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Context variables created with UpdateContext() are scoped to a single screen and cannot be accessed from other screens.
- C. Collections store tables of data, which is overkill for storing a single value.
- D. There are no local variables inside formulas in Power Apps; the concept does not exist in the Power Fx language.

questions.questionText: You are designing a model-driven app and need to organize the navigation so that users can access Cases, Contacts, and Accounts from the left navigation pane. What should you configure?
options: A. The app's site map with areas, groups, and subareas for each table.
B. Navigation buttons on each form.
C. Quick links on the dashboard.
D. A canvas app page embedded in the model-driven app.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. The site map in a model-driven app defines the navigation structure. You configure areas (top-level grouping), groups (sections within areas), and subareas (links to specific tables or dashboards) that appear in the left navigation pane.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Navigation buttons on forms are for navigating between related records, not for app-level navigation.
- C. Quick links on dashboards provide shortcuts but do not define the primary navigation structure.
- D. Embedded canvas pages add custom UI but are not used to define the navigation structure of a model-driven app.

questions.questionText: You need to ensure that sales representatives can only view and edit their own account records, but sales managers can view all account records in the business unit. What should you configure?
options: A. Create two security roles: one with User-level read/write for sales reps and one with Business Unit-level read for managers.
B. Create a single security role with Organization-level permissions.
C. Use field-level security to hide account records.
D. Create a personal view for each user.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. Dataverse security roles support access levels including User (own records only), Business Unit (all records in the BU), and Organization (all records). Creating two roles with appropriate levels meets both requirements.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Organization-level permissions would give everyone access to all records, which is too broad.
- C. Field-level security controls access to specific columns, not row-level record visibility.
- D. Personal views filter what users see in the UI but do not enforce security; users could still access records through other means.

questions.questionText: You are building a canvas app that needs to work on both tablets and phones. What approach should you use?
options: A. Build a single app with tablet layout and it will automatically resize for phones.
B. Build a responsive canvas app using containers with flexible width and height.
C. Build two separate apps - one for tablet and one for phone.
D. Use only the phone layout since it works on both devices.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Responsive canvas apps use layout containers (horizontal and vertical) with flexible sizing to automatically adapt to different screen sizes. This single-app approach provides optimal layouts for both tablets and phones.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Tablet layouts do not automatically resize properly for phone screens; controls may overlap or be too small.
- C. Building two separate apps doubles the maintenance effort and can lead to inconsistencies.
- D. Phone layouts on tablets waste screen space and do not take advantage of the larger display.

questions.questionText: Your Power Automate flow sometimes fails when an external API returns an error. You want the flow to retry the API call up to 3 times before failing. What should you configure?
options: A. Add a parallel branch that handles errors.
B. Configure the retry policy on the HTTP action to retry up to 3 times.
C. Add a Condition action to check for errors after the HTTP call.
D. Use a Try-Catch block in the flow.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Power Automate HTTP actions support configurable retry policies. You can set the retry policy type (fixed interval or exponential backoff) and the number of retries (up to 3 by default, configurable) in the action's settings.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Parallel branches run simultaneously, not as a retry mechanism.
- C. Checking for errors after the call does not retry the failed call automatically.
- D. Power Automate does not have a traditional Try-Catch block, though you can configure 'Run after' settings for error handling.

questions.questionText: You are building a canvas app that connects to Dataverse. Which connector should you use for the best performance and functionality?
options: A. The Dataverse (legacy) connector.
B. The Microsoft Dataverse connector.
C. The SQL Server connector pointed at the Dataverse TDS endpoint.
D. A custom connector to the Dataverse Web API.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Microsoft Dataverse connector (the current, non-legacy version) provides the best performance and full feature set for connecting canvas apps to Dataverse, including support for delegation, server-side filtering, and all data types.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. The Dataverse (legacy) connector has limited functionality and is being deprecated in favor of the current connector.
- C. Using the TDS endpoint via SQL connector adds unnecessary complexity and may not support all Dataverse features.
- D. A custom connector to the Web API is unnecessarily complex when a native connector is available.

questions.questionText: You need to create a dashboard in a model-driven app that shows a chart of cases by status alongside a list of recent cases. What type of dashboard should you create?
options: A. A Power BI embedded dashboard.
B. A classic system dashboard with a chart component and a view (list) component.
C. A canvas app embedded as a dashboard.
D. A SharePoint web part dashboard.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Classic system dashboards in model-driven apps support multiple components including charts, views (lists), web resources, and Power BI tiles. You can arrange a chart showing cases by status and a view showing recent cases side by side.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Power BI dashboards are excellent for advanced analytics but are not needed for a simple chart and list display.
- C. Embedding a canvas app as a dashboard is possible but overly complex for displaying a standard chart and list.
- D. SharePoint web parts are not a dashboard option within model-driven apps.

questions.questionText: You need your canvas app to allow users to take a photo with their device camera and save it to a SharePoint document library. Which control should you add to the app?
options: A. Image control.
B. Camera control.
C. Add picture control (media upload).
D. Pen input control.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Camera control in canvas apps activates the device camera and allows users to capture photos directly within the app. The captured photo (Camera1.Photo) can then be saved to SharePoint using the Power Apps SharePoint connector.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. The Image control displays images but does not capture photos from the camera.
- C. The Add picture control allows users to upload existing images from their device but does not directly access the camera.
- D. The Pen input control captures handwritten input or drawings, not photos.

questions.questionText: A user needs to create a flow that sends a push notification to their phone when they receive an email from their manager. They have no flow building experience. What is the fastest approach?
options: A. Build the flow from scratch using the blank flow template.
B. Use a pre-built flow template that matches the scenario and customize it.
C. Write a Power Automate expression for the notification.
D. Use a desktop flow to monitor Outlook.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Power Automate provides pre-built templates for common scenarios. Finding a template that matches (email notification from specific sender) and customizing it with the manager's email address is the fastest approach, especially for users without flow building experience.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Building from scratch takes longer and requires more knowledge of triggers and actions.
- C. Expressions are part of flow logic but you cannot write an expression as a standalone notification mechanism.
- D. Desktop flows are for automating desktop application interactions, not for email notifications.

questions.questionText: You need a column on the Project table that automatically shows the total number of tasks related to each project. What type of column should you create?
options: A. A calculated column using a formula.
B. A rollup column that counts related Task records.
C. A whole number column updated manually.
D. A Power Automate flow that updates the count on each change.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A rollup column in Dataverse can aggregate data from related records. A rollup configured to count Task records related to each Project automatically maintains the total count. Rollup columns are recalculated periodically by the system.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Calculated columns perform calculations on columns within the same record; they cannot aggregate data from related records in child tables.
- C. A manually updated column would quickly become inaccurate as tasks are added or removed.
- D. A Power Automate flow would work but is more complex and creates dependencies compared to the built-in rollup column.

questions.questionText: Field workers need to use a canvas app when they have no internet connectivity. You need to enable offline data access. What function should you use to cache data locally?
options: A. SaveData() and LoadData() functions.
B. Collect() function only.
C. Set() function for global variables.
D. Patch() function with offline mode.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. SaveData() saves a collection to the device's local storage, and LoadData() retrieves it. Together, they enable offline data access in canvas apps. You load data when the app starts (from local cache if offline) and save data locally for offline use.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Collect() stores data in memory but does not persist it to local device storage for true offline access.
- C. Set() stores a single value in a global variable in memory only.
- D. Patch() creates or updates records in a data source but does not have an offline mode parameter.

questions.questionText: You want to guide users through a standard sales process with stages: Qualify, Develop, Propose, and Close. Each stage should require specific fields to be completed before moving to the next stage. What should you create?
options: A. A Power Automate cloud flow with approval stages.
B. A business process flow with four stages and required steps.
C. A canvas app with four screens for each stage.
D. A series of business rules that enforce field requirements.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A business process flow creates a visual guide at the top of a model-driven app form showing the stages of a process. Each stage can have required steps (data fields) that must be completed before the user can advance to the next stage.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A cloud flow runs in the background and does not provide a visual stage-based guide on the form.
- C. A canvas app with screens would not integrate natively with the model-driven app's record management.
- D. Business rules enforce field-level logic but do not provide a visual stage-based process guide.

questions.questionText: You are using the SharePoint connector in a canvas app. Which two operations are supported by this connector? Each correct answer presents a valid operation.
options: A. Create and read items in SharePoint lists.
B. Manage SharePoint site permissions.
C. Update and delete items in SharePoint lists.
D. Manage SharePoint site collections.
E. Create new SharePoint sites.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A and C. The SharePoint connector supports CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on SharePoint list items and document library files. These are the primary data operations available through the connector in canvas apps.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Managing site permissions is an administrative action not available through the standard SharePoint connector.
- D. Managing site collections is a SharePoint administrative function not available through the connector.
- E. Creating new SharePoint sites requires administrative APIs not available through the canvas app SharePoint connector.

questions.topic: Power Automate Flows - Scope and Run After
questions.questionText: You need to implement error handling in your Power Automate flow so that if any action within a group of actions fails, a notification email is sent. What should you use?
options: A. Add a Condition after each action to check for failures.
B. Use a Scope action containing the group of actions, then add a notification action configured to 'Run after' the Scope has failed.
C. Use a Terminate action to stop the flow on error.
D. Add parallel branches for each possible error.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Scope action groups multiple actions together. You can then configure a subsequent action (like Send Email) with 'Run after' set to run when the Scope has failed. This implements a try-catch pattern in Power Automate.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Checking each action individually is tedious and does not scale well.
- C. Terminate stops the flow but does not send a notification before stopping.
- D. Parallel branches for each error would be extremely complex and impractical.

questions.questionText: You are using the Filter function on a SharePoint data source with 5,000 items. You receive a delegation warning. Which function is NOT delegable to SharePoint?
options: A. Filter with equals (=) comparison.
B. Filter with StartsWith function.
C. Filter using the in operator with a collection.
D. Sort by a column.
questions.explanation:
Correct: C. The 'in' operator comparing against a local collection is not delegable to SharePoint because the server cannot evaluate a comparison against a local client-side collection. This causes the delegation warning.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Filter with equals comparison (=) is delegable to SharePoint.
- B. StartsWith is delegable to SharePoint (though EndsWith and Substring are not).
- D. Sort by a single column is delegable to SharePoint.

questions.questionText: You need to add a chart to a model-driven app that shows the count of cases grouped by priority. Where do you create this chart?
options: A. In the canvas app designer.
B. In the model-driven app designer under the table's chart definitions.
C. In Power BI as an embedded report.
D. In Excel and import it as an image.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Charts in model-driven apps are defined as system charts associated with specific tables. You can create a chart for the Cases table that uses Priority as the category axis and Count as the aggregate function, directly in the chart editor.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Canvas app designer creates canvas apps, not model-driven app charts.
- C. While Power BI can be embedded, a simple count by priority chart is more efficiently created as a native system chart.
- D. Importing Excel charts as images creates static, non-interactive content.

questions.questionText: When you delete a parent Account record, you want all related Contact records to also be deleted automatically. What cascading behavior should you configure on the one-to-many relationship?
options: A. Cascade delete: Restrict (prevent deletion if children exist).
B. Cascade delete: Cascade All (delete all related children).
C. Cascade delete: Remove Link (clear the lookup, keep children).
D. Cascade delete: No cascade.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Setting the cascade delete behavior to 'Cascade All' automatically deletes all related child records (Contacts) when the parent record (Account) is deleted.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. 'Restrict' prevents the parent from being deleted if child records exist.
- C. 'Remove Link' clears the relationship but keeps the child records, leaving orphaned contacts.
- D. 'No cascade' does nothing to the child records, potentially leaving orphaned data.

questions.questionText: You have a header with a logo and navigation buttons that appears on every screen of your canvas app. Instead of duplicating it on each screen, what should you create?
options: A. A component library with a reusable header component.
B. A separate canvas app for the header.
C. A Power Automate flow that generates the header.
D. An image of the header inserted on each screen.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. A component (or component library) allows you to create a reusable UI element that can be placed on multiple screens. When you update the component, all instances across screens update automatically, ensuring consistency and reducing maintenance.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. A separate app for a header is not a supported or practical approach.
- C. Power Automate flows handle business logic, not UI rendering.
- D. An image is static, cannot contain interactive navigation buttons, and must be manually updated on each screen.

questions.questionText: You have common logic (like sending formatted notifications) that is used in multiple flows. To avoid duplicating this logic, what should you do?
options: A. Copy and paste the actions between flows.
B. Create a child flow with the common logic and call it from parent flows using the 'Run a Child Flow' action.
C. Create a custom connector for the notification logic.
D. Use a template that includes the common actions.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A child flow encapsulates reusable logic that can be called from multiple parent flows. The 'Run a Child Flow' action passes inputs to the child flow and receives outputs, promoting reuse and maintainability.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Copying actions creates duplicated logic that must be maintained separately in each flow.
- C. A custom connector is for external API integration, not for reusing flow logic.
- D. Templates help start new flows but do not provide runtime reuse of common logic across existing flows.

questions.questionText: You are creating a custom connector for an API that requires users to authenticate with their organizational Microsoft Entra ID credentials. Which authentication type should you configure?
options: A. API Key authentication.
B. OAuth 2.0 with Azure Active Directory.
C. Basic authentication (username and password).
D. Windows authentication (NTLM).
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. OAuth 2.0 with Azure Active Directory (Microsoft Entra ID) is the appropriate authentication type when users need to authenticate using their organizational credentials. This provides secure, standards-based authentication with token-based access.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. API Key authentication uses a static key, not individual user credentials.
- C. Basic authentication transmits credentials in base64 encoding, which is less secure and does not leverage organizational identity.
- D. Windows authentication (NTLM) is for on-premises Windows environments and is not supported in custom connectors.

questions.questionText: You need to create a new record in a Dataverse table from a canvas app without using a form control. Which function should you use?
options: A. Collect(Products, {Name: "New Product", Price: 29.99})
B. Patch(Products, Defaults(Products), {Name: "New Product", Price: 29.99})
C. Update(Products, {Name: "New Product", Price: 29.99})
D. SubmitForm(FormControl)
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Patch function with Defaults(tableName) as the second argument creates a new record in the data source. Defaults() provides the default values for a new record, and the third argument specifies the field values for the new record.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Collect() adds records to a local collection in memory but does not directly create a record in Dataverse.
- C. Update() replaces an existing record entirely; it does not create new records.
- D. SubmitForm() requires a form control, which the question specifically avoids.

questions.questionText: Users want to see a chronological view of all communications (emails, phone calls, notes, and appointments) related to a customer record. Which model-driven app control provides this?
options: A. A subgrid showing related activities.
B. The Timeline control on the form.
C. A canvas app embedded in the form.
D. A chart showing activity counts by date.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Timeline control is a built-in model-driven app control that displays a chronological list of all related activities (emails, phone calls, notes, appointments, tasks, etc.) on a record's form. Users can also create new activities directly from the timeline.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A subgrid shows related records in a table format but does not provide the chronological, conversation-like view of the timeline.
- C. An embedded canvas app would require custom development to replicate timeline functionality.
- D. A chart provides aggregate visualization, not a detailed chronological list of individual activities.

questions.questionText: Your app uses a SharePoint site URL that differs between development and production environments. What is the best way to manage this configuration?
options: A. Hard-code the URL in the app and change it before deploying to production.
B. Use a Dataverse environment variable to store the URL and reference it in the app.
C. Create a settings table in Dataverse and store the URL there.
D. Use an app setting file that is deployed separately.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Dataverse environment variables are designed specifically for configuration values that differ across environments. You define the variable with a default value and can set an environment-specific current value in each environment during solution deployment.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Hard-coding URLs and manually changing them is error-prone and does not follow ALM best practices.
- C. While a settings table works, environment variables are the purpose-built feature for this exact scenario.
- D. Power Apps does not support external app setting files in the traditional development sense.

questions.questionText: Your canvas app is loading slowly. Which two optimization techniques should you apply? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
options: A. Use Concurrent() to load multiple data sources in parallel on App.OnStart.
B. Add more screens to distribute the load.
C. Use DelayOutput on text input controls used for filtering.
D. Increase the delegation limit to 100,000.
E. Add more controls to each screen for visual appeal.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A and C. Using Concurrent() in App.OnStart loads multiple data sources simultaneously instead of sequentially, reducing startup time. DelayOutput on text inputs prevents the app from querying the data source on every keystroke, waiting until the user stops typing.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. Adding more screens does not improve loading speed; screens still need to be loaded when navigated to.
- D. Increasing the delegation limit would cause the app to retrieve more data locally, potentially making it slower.
- E. More controls increase rendering time and worsen performance.

questions.questionText: In a Power Automate flow, you need to extract just the year from a date value stored in a variable called 'OrderDate'. Which expression should you use?
options: A. formatDateTime(variables('OrderDate'), 'yyyy')
B. substring(variables('OrderDate'), 0, 4)
C. year(variables('OrderDate'))
D. split(variables('OrderDate'), '-')[0]
questions.explanation:
Correct: A. The formatDateTime() function with the 'yyyy' format string extracts the year from a date value as a string. This is the standard and reliable approach for date part extraction in Power Automate.

Why the others are wrong:
- B. substring() might work if the date is always in a specific string format, but it is fragile and not designed for date manipulation.
- C. While year() exists in some expression languages, Power Automate expressions do not have a standalone year() function.
- D. split() on a hyphen assumes a specific date format and is unreliable for date manipulation.

questions.questionText: You are using a Gallery control in a canvas app connected to a Dataverse table with over 50,000 records. Which function is delegable when filtering data from Dataverse?
options: A. Search()
B. Filter() with simple comparison operators
C. CountRows()
D. Last()
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Filter() with simple comparison operators (=, <>, <, >, <=, >=) and logical operators (And, Or) is delegable to Dataverse, allowing the server to process the query and return only matching records.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Search() is delegable for Dataverse and SharePoint, but Filter() with comparison operators is the most broadly delegable standard approach.
- C. CountRows() is not delegable for most data sources and will only count records up to the delegation limit.
- D. Last() is not delegable and only operates on locally loaded data.

questions.questionText: You need to automatically set the Priority field to 'High' when the Estimated Revenue field is greater than $100,000 in a model-driven app. What should you use?
options: A. A JavaScript web resource.
B. A business rule with a condition and Set Field Value action.
C. A canvas app embedded in the form.
D. A Power Automate cloud flow.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A business rule can evaluate conditions (Estimated Revenue > 100,000) and perform actions like Set Field Value (Priority = High) without code. Business rules run on both the client (form) and server side.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. JavaScript web resources can accomplish this but are unnecessary complexity for simple conditional logic that business rules handle natively.
- C. An embedded canvas app is not the appropriate tool for field-level conditional logic.
- D. A cloud flow runs asynchronously and would not provide the immediate form-level response users expect.

questions.questionText: You need to create a column in Dataverse where users can select multiple values from a predefined list, such as selecting multiple product categories. Which column type should you use?
options: A. Lookup column.
B. Choices (multi-select option set) column.
C. Single line of text column.
D. Yes/No (Boolean) column.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A Choices (multi-select option set) column allows users to select one or more values from a predefined list, which is exactly what is needed for selecting multiple product categories.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A lookup column references another table and only allows single selection by default; for multi-select you would need a many-to-many relationship.
- C. A single line of text does not provide a predefined list or structured multi-select capability.
- D. A Yes/No column only supports two states and cannot represent multiple categories.

questions.questionText: You need to create an approval workflow where a document must be approved by both a manager AND a department head before proceeding. Which approval type should you use?
options: A. Approve/Reject - First to respond.
B. Approve/Reject - Everyone must approve.
C. Custom Responses - Wait for one response.
D. Approve/Reject - First to respond, with two separate approval actions.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. 'Approve/Reject - Everyone must approve' sends the approval to all specified approvers and waits until every approver responds. The approval only proceeds if all approvers approve.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. 'First to respond' completes as soon as any one approver responds, not requiring all approvers.
- C. 'Custom Responses - Wait for one response' waits for only one response with custom options.
- D. Two separate 'First to respond' actions would work sequentially but is not the standard pattern for parallel all-must-approve scenarios.

questions.questionText: In a canvas app, you want to store data temporarily in memory for use across multiple screens without repeatedly querying the data source. What should you use?
options: A. A global variable set with Set().
B. A collection created with ClearCollect() or Collect().
C. A context variable set with UpdateContext().
D. A text input control.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Collections are in-memory tables that can store multiple records and columns, accessible across all screens, making them ideal for caching data from a data source.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A global variable (Set) stores a single value, not a table of records.
- C. A context variable is scoped to a single screen and cannot be accessed from other screens.
- D. A text input control is a UI element, not a data storage mechanism.

questions.questionText: In a model-driven app, users need to see different subsets of data based on their role. How should you configure this?
options: A. Create separate apps for each role.
B. Create multiple system views or personal views with appropriate filters.
C. Export the data to separate Excel files.
D. Use JavaScript to hide rows.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. System views with appropriate filter criteria can be configured to show different subsets of data. Combined with security roles, this provides role-based data visibility within the same app.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Creating separate apps increases maintenance overhead and is unnecessary when views can filter data.
- C. Exporting to Excel removes data from the app experience and is not dynamic.
- D. JavaScript to hide rows is unreliable, not supported, and does not actually secure the data.

questions.questionText: You need to automatically generate a unique reference number (like 'CASE-0001', 'CASE-0002') for each new record in a Dataverse table. Which column type should you use?
options: A. A whole number column with a business rule.
B. An autonumber column with a custom format.
C. A calculated column.
D. A text column filled by a Power Automate flow.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Dataverse autonumber columns automatically generate sequential, unique values using configurable formats (prefix, seed value, number of digits) like 'CASE-{SEQNUM:4}' producing CASE-0001, CASE-0002, etc.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A whole number with a business rule cannot reliably generate unique sequential numbers, especially with concurrent record creation.
- C. Calculated columns derive values from other fields but cannot generate sequential numbers.
- D. While a flow could set a value, it adds unnecessary complexity and latency compared to the built-in autonumber feature.

questions.questionText: Which two trigger types are available in Power Automate cloud flows? (Choose two.)
options: A. Automated triggers that respond to events like a new email or record creation.
B. Scheduled triggers that run at specified intervals.
C. Voice-activated triggers using a microphone.
D. Gravity-based triggers using device sensors.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A and B. Automated triggers respond to events (new email, record created, file uploaded, etc.) and scheduled triggers run on a recurring schedule (every hour, daily, weekly).

Why the others are wrong:
- C. Voice-activated triggers are not a standard Power Automate trigger type.
- D. Gravity-based triggers do not exist in Power Automate.

questions.questionText: You are building a canvas app that must work well on both tablets and phones. What feature should you use to ensure the app adapts to different screen sizes?
options: A. Create two completely separate apps.
B. Use responsive containers and layout properties to adapt to different screen sizes.
C. Use only fixed-size controls.
D. Set all controls to be invisible on phones.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Responsive containers (horizontal and vertical containers) and layout properties allow controls to resize and reflow based on available screen space, creating apps that work across device sizes.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Maintaining two apps doubles development and maintenance effort.
- C. Fixed-size controls do not adapt to different screen sizes.
- D. Hiding controls on phones removes functionality rather than adapting the layout.

questions.questionText: In a model-driven app, which type of dashboard allows users to create their own personalized view of data?
options: A. System dashboard.
B. Personal dashboard.
C. Power BI embedded dashboard.
D. Canvas app dashboard.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Personal dashboards are created by individual users and are only visible to the creator. They allow users to customize their own view of charts, lists, and other components.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. System dashboards are created by administrators or customizers and shared with all users who have access; they are not personalized per user.
- C. Power BI embedded dashboards show Power BI reports but are not the standard personal dashboard mechanism in model-driven apps.
- D. Canvas app dashboards are embedded canvas apps, not the personal dashboard feature.

questions.questionText: You have an Account table with a one-to-many relationship to an Opportunities table. You need a column on the Account that automatically shows the total value of all related opportunities. Which column type should you use?
options: A. A calculated column.
B. A rollup column with SUM aggregation.
C. A currency column updated manually.
D. A formula column.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A rollup column aggregates data from related records using functions like SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, or MAX. A rollup on the Account table that SUMs the value from related Opportunities automatically calculates the total.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Calculated columns derive values from fields on the same record, not from related records.
- C. Manual updates are error-prone and do not automatically reflect changes in opportunities.
- D. Formula columns (Power Fx) currently have limited support for cross-table aggregation compared to rollup columns.

questions.questionText: In a Power Automate flow, you need to send different emails based on whether an invoice amount is above or below $5,000. Which control action should you use?
options: A. Apply to each.
B. Condition (If/Else).
C. Switch.
D. Do until.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Condition action evaluates a true/false expression (Invoice Amount > 5000) and branches into Yes and No paths, allowing different actions for each outcome.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Apply to each iterates over an array of items, not for conditional branching.
- C. Switch evaluates against multiple specific values, not a greater-than/less-than comparison.
- D. Do until repeats actions until a condition is met, not for simple branching logic.

questions.questionText: You have created a custom header that should appear consistently across 15 screens in your canvas app. What is the best approach to implement this?
options: A. Copy and paste the header controls onto each screen.
B. Create a component library with the header component and use it on each screen.
C. Use a gallery control as the header.
D. Create a separate app for the header.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Component libraries allow you to create reusable UI components. A header component can be designed once and added to all screens, ensuring consistency and making updates propagate to all instances.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Copy-pasting creates 15 independent copies that must be updated individually when changes are needed.
- C. A gallery is designed for displaying lists of data, not as a static header component.
- D. A separate app cannot serve as a header within another app.

questions.questionText: A user needs to be able to read all records in a Dataverse table regardless of ownership, but should only be able to edit records they own. At what access level should the Read privilege be set in the security role?
options: A. User level.
B. Organization level.
C. Business Unit level.
D. None.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Organization-level Read access allows the user to read all records across the entire organization. The Write/Update privilege would remain at User level, restricting editing to records they own.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. User-level Read would only allow reading records they own, not all records.
- C. Business Unit level would only allow reading records owned by users in the same business unit.
- D. None would prevent the user from reading any records.

questions.questionText: You want to group multiple actions in a Power Automate flow so that if any action in the group fails, you can run error-handling actions. What should you use?
options: A. A Compose action.
B. A Scope action with a subsequent Scope configured to run on failure.
C. A Terminate action.
D. A Delay action.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Scope actions group multiple actions together. A second Scope configured with 'Run after' set to 'has failed' acts as a catch block, running only when any action in the first Scope fails.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Compose is for data manipulation, not for grouping or error handling.
- C. Terminate ends the flow entirely rather than handling the error.
- D. Delay pauses the flow for a specified duration and has no error-handling capability.

questions.questionText: You want to display a list of products in a canvas app where each product shows an image, name, and price. Which control is best suited for this?
options: A. A Data Table control.
B. A Gallery control with a custom template.
C. A Form control.
D. A Label control.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. A Gallery control displays a repeating template for each record, allowing you to combine images, labels, and other controls in a rich, customizable layout for each product.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A Data Table displays data in a simple tabular grid format and has limited support for images and custom layouts.
- C. A Form control is designed for viewing or editing a single record, not displaying a list.
- D. A Label shows a single text value and cannot display a list of records.

questions.questionText: In a model-driven app, you need to create a chart that shows the count of open cases grouped by priority. Where should you create this chart?
options: A. In Power BI Desktop.
B. In the chart designer within the model-driven app or solution explorer.
C. In Excel.
D. In a canvas app.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Model-driven apps include a built-in chart designer where you can create system charts (or users can create personal charts) that aggregate and visualize Dataverse data directly within the app experience.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Power BI Desktop creates external reports that can be embedded but are not native model-driven app charts.
- C. Excel is external and does not integrate as a native chart in the model-driven app.
- D. Canvas apps are separate applications and not the mechanism for model-driven app charts.

questions.questionText: You are integrating an external system with Dataverse and need to upsert (update or create) records based on an external ID rather than the Dataverse GUID. What should you configure?
options: A. A calculated column.
B. An alternate key on the external ID column.
C. A rollup column.
D. A virtual table.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Alternate keys define unique constraints on one or more columns, enabling upsert operations using the alternate key values instead of the primary key GUID. This is essential for integration scenarios where external systems use their own identifiers.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Calculated columns derive values and cannot serve as unique identifiers for upsert.
- C. Rollup columns aggregate related data and are not used for record identification.
- D. Virtual tables display external data in Dataverse but do not help with upserting into Dataverse tables.

questions.questionText: You have a Power Automate flow that connects to different SharePoint sites in development and production environments. How should you configure the flow to easily switch between sites?
options: A. Hard-code the SharePoint site URL in each action.
B. Use environment variables to store the SharePoint site URL and reference them in the flow.
C. Create separate flows for each environment.
D. Use a text input trigger parameter each time.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Environment variables store configuration values that differ between environments. The flow references the variable, and the value is set per-environment, allowing seamless deployment without modifying the flow.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Hard-coded URLs require modifying the flow for each environment.
- C. Separate flows double maintenance effort and introduce inconsistency risk.
- D. A trigger input requires manual entry each run, which is impractical for automated flows.

questions.questionText: In a canvas app, you want to navigate to a details screen and pass the selected record from a gallery to the form on the details screen. Which function and approach should you use?
options: A. Navigate(DetailsScreen, ScreenTransition.None) and use Gallery.Selected on the form.
B. Launch(DetailsScreen) with the record as a parameter.
C. Set(SelectedRecord, Gallery.Selected) and then Navigate(DetailsScreen).
D. Both A and C are valid approaches.
questions.explanation:
Correct: D. Both approaches work. You can use Gallery.Selected directly in the form's Item property on the target screen (approach A), or store the selection in a global variable first and reference it on the target screen (approach C). Both are common patterns.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. This is valid but not the only approach.
- B. Launch() opens an external URL or another app, not a screen within the same app.
- C. This is valid but not the only approach.

questions.questionText: Which two form types are available in model-driven apps? (Choose two.)
options: A. Main form.
B. Quick create form.
C. Canvas form.
D. Gallery form.
questions.explanation:
Correct: A and B. Main forms are the primary data entry forms in model-driven apps. Quick create forms provide a simplified form for rapid data entry, typically appearing as a flyout panel.

Why the others are wrong:
- C. Canvas forms exist in canvas apps, not as a form type in model-driven apps (though canvas apps can be embedded).
- D. Gallery forms do not exist as a form type in model-driven apps.

questions.questionText: You need to import 500 records from an Excel file into a Dataverse table. What is the simplest built-in method?
options: A. Write a custom plug-in.
B. Use the Import from Excel feature in the Power Apps maker portal.
C. Type each record manually.
D. Use Azure Data Factory.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Power Apps maker portal provides a built-in Import from Excel feature that maps Excel columns to Dataverse table columns and imports the data directly. This is the simplest approach for moderate data volumes.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. A custom plug-in is over-engineered for a simple data import task.
- C. Manual data entry for 500 records is extremely time-consuming and error-prone.
- D. Azure Data Factory is powerful but excessive for a simple 500-record Excel import.

questions.questionText: You have common logic that needs to be reused across multiple Power Automate cloud flows. What is the recommended approach?
options: A. Copy the common actions into each flow.
B. Create a child flow (solution-aware) and call it using the 'Run a Child Flow' action.
C. Use a shared Excel spreadsheet to coordinate flows.
D. Combine all flows into one large flow.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. Child flows encapsulate reusable logic in a separate flow that can be called from parent flows, promoting reuse and easier maintenance. They must be solution-aware to use the 'Run a Child Flow' action.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Copying actions creates multiple copies that must be updated independently.
- C. An Excel spreadsheet cannot coordinate flow logic.
- D. A single large flow becomes difficult to maintain and troubleshoot.

questions.questionText: You need to build a canvas app that field workers can use in areas with intermittent connectivity. How can you enable offline data access?
options: A. Canvas apps cannot work offline.
B. Use LoadData() and SaveData() functions to cache data locally on the device.
C. Use a VPN connection.
D. Download the data to a USB drive.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. LoadData() and SaveData() functions allow canvas apps to store and retrieve data from the local device cache, enabling offline scenarios. Data can be synced when connectivity is restored.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Canvas apps do support offline capabilities using local caching functions.
- C. A VPN requires network connectivity and does not solve intermittent connectivity issues.
- D. USB drives are not a supported data access mechanism for canvas apps.

questions.questionText: What does the Timeline control display in a model-driven app form?
options: A. A Gantt chart of project tasks.
B. A chronological view of activities (notes, emails, phone calls, appointments) related to the record.
C. A version history of the record's changes.
D. A calendar view of upcoming events.
questions.explanation:
Correct: B. The Timeline control shows a chronological list of related activities including notes, posts, emails, phone calls, appointments, and tasks associated with the current record, providing a communication history.

Why the others are wrong:
- A. Gantt charts are not part of the Timeline control; they are used in project management tools.
- C. Record change history is accessed through audit logs, not the Timeline control.
- D. Calendar views are separate from the Timeline control, which shows past and current activities.
